title: Short spells of extreme heat during pregnancy linked to higher newborn health risks, study finds - Prittle Prattle News
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Bihar
full_text:
Even three to four consecutive days of extreme heat during pregnancy can increase the likelihood of babies being born with low birth weight, according to a nationwide study that examined more than 209,000 births across India.
The study, titled Effect of extreme heat stress on adverse pregnancy outcomes in India, analysed 209,266 births recorded between 2015 and 2020. Researchers matched each birth with local heat conditions experienced throughout pregnancy, measuring exposure through consecutive “sweltering” days based on the India Heat Index rather than average temperatures.
Researchers also found that the stage of pregnancy during which heat exposure occurred influenced birth outcomes. Extreme heat during the first trimester was associated with a higher risk of preterm birth, while exposure during the second trimester was linked to low birth weight.
“While the per-day increase is small, it compounds over a long, hot pregnancy and affects a very large population, making it a meaningful public health concern in a country where extreme heat is becoming more common,” said Vineetha Vincent, lead author of the study from UQIDAR.
“The mothers most exposed to extreme heat are often those with the fewest resources to cope with it: the poorest, the least educated, the under nourished. Any adaptation response that ignores this social gradient will miss the women who need protection most,” said Professor Sagnik Dey, co author of the study from IIT Delhi.
